pattern: Counting strictly increasing subsequences restricted to multiples of each g gives the gcd-divisible totals, and a divisor-sieve subtraction peels those down to exact-gcd counts weighted by g.
hints: Counting strictly increasing subsequences whose gcd is exactly g is hard directly, but counting those whose every element is a multiple of g is easy with a Fenwick tree over values.

For each g, restrict to array elements divisible by g (in original order) and count all non-empty strictly increasing subsequences; that over-counts, since a gcd that is a true multiple of g also qualifies.
Go from large g down to small, subtracting the already-computed exact counts of every proper multiple of g to isolate the exact-gcd count, then add g times that count to the answer modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: single element is its own gcd
duplicates cannot extend a strict subsequence
coprime increasing values keep gcd one

stress.description: moderate array over a small value range so many subsequences and gcds appear
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 60) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
stress.note: size kept modest so the quadratic gcd-DP oracle stays fast

complexity.time: O(maxV log maxV + n log n per divisor)
